paragraphs.text: SRD-46 has a neat rare-earth story: **lanthanides behave similarly overall, but the database strongly highlights their preference for hard, oxygen-rich ligands**.

claims.text: SRD-46 has a neat rare-earth story:

the database strongly highlights their preference for hard, oxygen-rich ligands

paragraphs.text:
### A striking example from SRD-46
One broad rare-earth ligand series is **(1,1-dimethylethylene)dinitrilotetraacetic acid** (`ligand_6286`), an **EDTA-type aminopolycarboxylate**.

| Property | SRD-46 result |
|---|---|
| Ligand | (1,1-Dimethylethylene)dinitrilotetraacetic acid |
| Ligand class | EDTA and derivatives |
| Formula | C12H20N2O8 |
| HxL definition | H4L |
| Rare-earth coverage | Includes La, Ce, Nd, Pr, Sm, Eu, Gd, Tb, Tm, Yb, Lu, Dy, Er, Ho, and Y |
| Total metal partners | 26 |
| Total measurements | 29 |

That’s interesting because **one ligand has been measured across almost the whole lanthanide series**, which is exactly what chemists do when they want to probe subtle rare-earth trends.

paragraphs.text:
### Broader rare-earth pattern in SRD-46
The richest rare-earth systems found were mostly **oxygen-donor ligands**:

| Ligand | Ligand type | Rare-earth pattern |
|---|---|---|
| ligand_8669: (1R,3R,4R,5R)-(-)-1,3,4,5-tetrahydroxycyclohexane-1-carboxylic acid | Polyhydroxy carboxylate | Broad lanthanide coverage; richest comparative set |
| ligand_9358: 1,2-dihydroxybenzene-3,5-disulfonic acid | Catechol-disulfonate | Strong data density, especially for heavier lanthanides |
| ligand_6333: (2-hydroxytrimethylene)dinitrilotetraacetic acid | Aminopolycarboxylate | Broad series coverage |
| ligand_6286: (1,1-dimethylethylene)dinitrilotetraacetic acid | EDTA derivative | Broad series coverage |
| ligand_8786: (Ethylthio)acetic acid | S-containing ligand | Present, but less richly covered |

paragraphs.text:
### Why this is chemically interesting
| Observation | Meaning |
|---|---|
| O-donor ligands dominate the rare-earth records | Lanthanide ions are **hard Lewis acids**, so they favor oxygen donors strongly |
| The same ligands recur from La to Lu | Rare earths are chemically similar enough for true series-wide comparison |
| S-donor ligands are rarer | Sulfur is a softer donor, so it is usually less favored by Ln³⁺ ions |
| A few ligands have extra thermodynamic data beyond logK | Those are especially valuable for understanding *why* binding is favorable |

paragraphs.text:
### One especially unusual detail
SRD-46 indicates that **(ethylenedithio)diacetic acid** (`ligand_9015`) stands out because it has not only stability constants but also **ΔH and ΔS** data, and only for a few rare earths (**Dy, Er, Sm**). That makes it a rare mechanistic window into lanthanide binding, not just a strength comparison.

That makes it a rare mechanistic window into lanthanide binding
not just a strength comparison.

paragraphs.text:
### Bottom line
The most interesting rare-earth takeaway from SRD-46 is:

| Takeaway | Interpretation |
|---|---|
| Rare earths look similar, but their coordination chemistry is highly systematic | Perfect for series-wide comparison studies |
| The database is dominated by oxygen-rich ligands for Ln³⁺ | Strong evidence of classic hard-acid behavior |
| Some ligands were studied across nearly the full lanthanide series | Useful for showing subtle lanthanide-contraction effects |

If you want, I can next pull **an actual La→Lu stability-constant trend table** for one ligand and show how binding strength changes across the series from SRD-46.

| The database is dominated by oxygen-rich ligands for Ln³⁺ | Strong evidence of classic hard-acid behavior |
| Some ligands were studied across nearly the full lanthanide series | Useful for showing subtle lanthanide-contraction effects |
If you want, I can next pull **an actual La→Lu stability-constant trend table** for one ligand and show how binding strength changes across the series
from SRD-46.
